aliases: Pasta with Parma Ham and Cheese

english: Pasta with Parma Ham and Cheese

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for fettuccine with Parma ham; broader 'pasta dish' term used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers pasta dishes broadly; no specific fettuccine-with-cured-ham code exists. Broader category used as defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific pasta preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of fettuccine with Parma ham weighs approximately 350 g, providing around 750 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy: Pasta cooked (FDC ID 168936), Prosciutto/cured ham (FDC ID 167903), Parmesan cheese (FDC ID 171241), and related component ingredient references
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for fettuccine with Parma ham as a composite dish. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked fettuccine (FDC ID 168936), prosciutto/cured ham (FDC ID 167903), Parmesan cheese (FDC ID 171241), Pecorino-style hard cheese, olive oil, and white wine, proportioned to reflect a typical restaurant preparation. The input record's per-100g values (sourced from GrabFood, confidence 0.7) are consistent with this USDA component composite estimate and have been retained with cross-validation. Sodium at 514 mg/100g is plausible given the cured ham and aged cheese content. GrabFood is not used as a primary nutrient source; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data is the authoritative basis.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fettuccine with Parma ham as a composite dish. GI of 52 is estimated using the analogous food 'fettuccine, cooked' (GI ~32–47, mean ~40 per University of Sydney GI Database) adjusted upward to ~52 to account for the fat and protein from Parma ham and cheese, which moderately slow gastric emptying, while the refined pasta base dominates the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (52) × available carbohydrate per serving (50 g from 350 g × 14.29 g/100g) / 100 = 26.0. GI category set to 'low' reflecting the pasta-dominant GI value; however, the large portion size elevates GL to a clinically significant level.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from cured Parma ham and aged Parmesan and Pecorino cheeses, with sodium at 514 mg per 100 g and approximately 1,800 mg per standard 350 g serving.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium load (~1,800 mg per serving, approaching the entire daily recommended limit of 2,000 mg); if consumed, request reduced portions of Parma ham and cheese, avoid additional salt, and pair with potassium-rich vegetables to partially offset the sodium burden.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined fettuccine pasta provides 14.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 0.86 g dietary fibre, yielding a moderate glycaemic response; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 50 g of available carbohydrate with a glycaemic load of ~26.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request whole-wheat fettuccine to increase fibre and lower the glycaemic index; the fat and protein from Parma ham and cheese moderately slow gastric emptying, but the refined pasta base and high glycaemic load per full serving make this dish a moderate-to-high glycaemic risk at restaurant portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 5.71 g per 100 g (approximately 20 g per serving) and sodium at 514 mg per 100 g (~1,800 mg per serving), combined with trans fat at 0.14 g per 100 g and cholesterol at 34.29 mg per 100 g, present a compounded cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish only occasionally and in reduced portions, as the combination of high saturated fat from aged cheeses, trans fat, high sodium from cured Parma ham, and cholesterol collectively elevates LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk; substituting with a smaller portion, leaner protein, and reduced cheese would meaningfully lower the cardiovascular burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at 5.71 g per 100 g exceeds the 5 g per 100 g threshold for RA concern; Parma ham is a processed cured meat contributing arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies inflammatory pathways.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should request a reduced portion of Parma ham or substitute with grilled chicken, and ask for a lighter application of Parmesan and Pecorino; the thyme and olive oil in the dish provide beneficial anti-inflammatory polyphenols (oleocanthal, rosmarinic acid) and should be retained.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fettuccine with Parma Ham (Fettuccine e Parma) is an Italian pasta dish served in Singapore restaurants, consisting of homemade egg fettuccine ribbons tossed with thinly sliced Parma ham (prosciutto di Parma), fresh thyme, Pinot Grigio white wine, and a blend of aged Parmesan and Pecorino Romano cheeses. The pasta is boiled al dente, then pan-tossed with the cured ham and aromatics in a light wine reduction, with the cheeses melted in to create a silky, savoury coating rather than a heavy cream sauce. The flavour profile is umami-rich, salty, and subtly herbaceous, with the nuttiness of aged Parmesan and the sharper tang of Pecorino complementing the delicate sweetness of the Parma ham. Texturally, the fettuccine is tender with slight bite, and the ham provides occasional chewiness. Nutritionally, a 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 750 kcal, 35 g protein, 45 g fat (20 g saturated), and 50 g carbohydrate. Sodium is high at approximately 1,800 mg per serving due to the cured ham and aged cheeses, making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The refined pasta base yields a moderate glycaemic load (~26 per serving), which is clinically relevant for type 2 diabetes management. The dish is not suitable for halal, vegetarian, or vegan diets. It contains gluten (pasta), dairy (Parmesan, Pecorino), and eggs (fresh pasta). This dish is commonly found on Italian restaurant menus in Singapore and is representative of Italian-style pasta preparations adapted for the Southeast Asian dining market.
common_user_queries: Is fettuccine with Parma ham healthy?
How many calories in fettuccine e Parma?

How much protein is in Parma ham pasta?
What is the glycemic index of fettuccine pasta?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from a USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component composite of cooked fettuccine, prosciutto, Parmesan, Pecorino, olive oil, and white wine. No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. The input record's GrabFood-sourced values (confidence 0.7) are consistent with the USDA component estimate and have been retained with cross-validation. GI is an analogous estimate based on cooked fettuccine GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite pasta dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central as a single entry; component-level fatty acid data was not aggregated due to preparation variability. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish preparation is not reported in the input record and was not reliably derivable from the USDA component approach without introducing unacceptable estimation error. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the input record and is not available from a single approved-source entry. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail for this composite dish is not available from a single approved-source entry in USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed as effectively zero for this preparation; the dish contains no significant vitamin C sources (pasta, cured ham, aged cheese, wine reduction). trans_fat_g retained at 0.14 g per 100 g from input record, consistent with trace trans fat from dairy-derived sources (Parmesan, Pecorino). This is an Italian-origin dish served in Singapore; local_names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi have been provided as descriptive transliterations/translations to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for dishes served in Singapore. GI category is set to 'low' (GI 52) per the University of Sydney classification thresholds (low: ≤55); however, the glycaemic load per 350 g serving is 26.0, which is high and clinically significant for diabetes management. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component composite; GrabFood is not used as a primary nutrient source.
